sentences.text: [INAUDIBLE] and the interviewer is Chana Meier.
That summer in Lodz, when we met our uncle Alexander and established more or less a life, Lodz was not bombed out during the war as Warsaw was.
So that there were all kinds of people returning, and all kinds of people trying to find their relatives or friends or whatever.
Anyhow, as I said, we lived with Uncle Alexander.
But he left for Sweden, because his wife was taken from one of the camps to Sweden.
So he left us.
Meanwhile, my sister, Krys married Miles Lerman.
And my sister, Anna, the oldest one, went to Warsaw, to a school of-- the Polish school of diplomacy.
And we-- eventually, Miles decided that-- they sort of adopted me.
Miles and Krys adopted me.
And they decided that I was going to stay with them until I get married, which, in fact, happened.
But Miles decided, and Krys that we would be leaving Poland.
And we went to Berlin, which at that time, set up a DP camp.
Now we left for Berlin early on in 1946.
And as soon as we arrived, we discovered that there was a school for all the kids who came through the camps.
And I was enrolled in the high school.
This time, everything was-- the courses were given in Hebrew.
[LAUGHS] So I came out of the Polish high school, and now I now had something to show for, because I just, you know, I spent in the Polish high school about seven months now, or six, whatever.
Anyhow, Berlin was wonderful for me, because we were very pampered.
All these kids that were coming in were really looked after, not only classes.
We were taken on excursions.
We were given extra chocolate.
We had our own dining room, you know.
Every effort was made to bring us out of that of-- of that experience, of the horror of the war that we came through.
And we signed up for the United States, Miles, Krys, and I.
And by early of 1947, we left the Berlin camp.
And I left the school with, with a report card of excellence and having made wonderful friends, whom I certainly have met later in America.
And we came to America, Miles, Krys, and I, on the boat, on February 11, 1947.
We were met at the boat by Miles's old aunt, who was 80 years old and her older husband.
And they asked us if-- they took us to their apartment on the Lower East Side and showed us how to pull the string on the water tank for the toilet because-- and how to put the electricity on.
And I thought that was very cute, because they left Poland about 80 years ago.
And as soon as we got settled, I went to high school.
I'm still in high school.
[LAUGHS] First it was the Polish high school, then it was the Hebrew high school.
Now it's the American high school.
But this high school is for real.

And this was Thomas Jefferson High School in Brooklyn, where they established a special course for kids who had an education in another language, you see, who had qualified for high school, but by virtue of not knowing the language, weren't really qualified.
So that we were enrolled in the regular courses plus a special course for the-- it was called Basic English, which was basically just orienting us to the educational system and so on.
And this-- I entered the school in 1947, graduated from high school in 1949, went to college, started Brooklyn College.
And I was recommended by a professor from Brooklyn College to become an instructor of Polish language at Indiana University, because they were looking for somebody.
And of course, having had my education interrupted all these times and, and having always hungered for more books and more knowledge, I said, no, I can't go.
Because I'm finally going to finish college.
He said, no, don't worry.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] They'll accommodate you, and they would let you finish your own work-- your own-- get your own degree, which is exactly what happened.
Because within those three years that I spent in Indiana, they allowed me to teach three hours a day doing the regular course and six hours in the summer.
So it worked out very well.
Of course, paid my tuition, and I was earning money.
I was very happy.
I was self-support-- sustaining and self-supporting.
And I was by then engaged to Victor, whom I met in New York, before I went to Indiana.
And at that same time, Victor was in the army.
It was the Korean War.
So that when I graduated-- and I graduated with honors, by the way, [LAUGHS] both from high school and college.
So that when I graduated from college in 1953, in February, and Victor was-- my husband, Victor, was discharged from the army in January.
We both came back to New York, and we got married in March of the same year, 1953.
And subsequently, we had two children.
One, his name is Harry.
Harry's a lawyer now and married to Cheryl, Cheryl Kohn.
And they have a little boy named Max.
He's just six years old now, sweetheart.
And then we have a younger son who is Paul.
And Paul is a geologist by training and lives in Albuquerque, New Mexico.
And we talk on the phone every day, but we see each other a few times a year.
And we're very close.
Of course, I'm very-- still very, very close to my surviving-- my sisters, with whom I survived.
And as I said, from the extended family, there were just practically nobody, and this was a very, very big family.
And as far as later years, I became a translator and interpreter, Polish into English, English into Polish.
And I've done a lot of work, commercial work and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] work for lawyers and, and all kinds of other work.
But basically, what it boils down to is that I'm forever involved in Holocaust research material of books.
As a matter of fact, right at this moment, I'm translating a book written in Polish about the Krakow ghetto.
And I collaborated on a Lodz ghetto book and a film also.
And somehow or other, for the last, oh, I would say about eight years, I am always asked to be doing material relating to the Holocaust.
Yes, I went back twice.
One time I went with Victor for just a visit, to show him around.
And, of course, we went to Auschwitz and to my hometown.
And we did a few other things that were of interest to me, such as the life of Chopin, because I worked on books about Chopin too.
But that's not why, I adore his music.
So we traveled the route of Chopin in Poland.

And the second time, I was without Victor, because I went on an interpreting mission for the United States Holocaust Memorial Museum, where they were negotiating to-- the release of the archives, Polish archives.
And this was 1987, and I was there as the interpreter for the representatives from the museum.
And that was a very interesting trip, because it was on the highest level.
And of course, after 1987, a lot of material was released for the museum.
And so those were the two times.

Well, when I went back to my hometown, I went to visit my parents' dearest friends, who really were quite instrumental in, in-- indirectly saving our lives, because they were sending stuff in for us while we were in the Starachowice camp.
And they would-- the husband of one of these ladies worked in the factory, munitions factory.
And he used to bring in things for us, through the factory.
But these people were dearest people to my parents and dearest to me.
As far as the others are concerned, there were all kinds of, of anti-Semitic incidents right after the war.
But certainly when I came in 1980, there was nothing for me.
I was by then an American, and I traveled as an American, with an American husband.
And there was no such thing as being either singled out or involved with anything of that sort, you see.
And--

Well, you see, my only hope through my experience was that after people have finally learned the truth about genocide on a scale that is unprecedented, you know, that a lesson would have been learned somewhere along the way.
That the inhumanity, this, this kind of, of disregarding the, the, the, the life of the other person has to be one way or another made-- has to be made understood, you see.
When I talk about my experiences, I always talk, hoping that people get a message from it.

What you have learned from the Holocaust, and I am not even talking about the final killing itself, but what leads up to it, from the first incident, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the string of every possible inhuman act that a person is capable of.
And I'm not just talking about taking a human life and burning him in the crematorium.
But I'm talking about the viciousness of people, of, of, of those who decide to be in power, you know.
The disregard of human life.
The, the total bestiality-- I mean, the animal in the person that is capable of doing that.
And how, how the world is ever going to know it, I don't know.
Because 50 years later, you know, the celebration of the Holocaust-- the liberation of Auschwitz was just being commemorated in Washington.

And I was there, as a matter of fact, because I served as interpreter for one of the keynote speakers, Ambassador [POLISH] who spoke at that meeting, and I was his interpreter and-- so I attended, I was, I attended the commemoration of Raoul Wallenberg, which you have now the other end of the coin-- the goodness, the absolute goodness of a human being, as against all this horror of the Nazi.
Certainly.
Vic?
Yeah, I would have a way.
I'll come around.
Yes.
Well, thank you for coming up.
I think it's very important that the type of record that's being made of my wife and other people that you do is important.
I think that the documentation is something that will go on for beyond our time.
And it would be wonderful that it is available to people to review what went on during the time my wife went through the Holocaust.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I want to correct my wife on one point.
When we were engaged, we were not engaged in 1950 before you went to Indiana University.
What happened was I was in the army, and she was out there.
And I was coming to New York, and so was she on a certain weekend.
And I said, meet me in front of a certain jewelry store, the name of which I'll leave nameless.
And we met there, and I said--
Oh, yes.
I said, I want to go in here for something.
And it was right in there that I bought her engagement ring.
That's right
And she walked out with a ring-- wearing that ring.
And that was it.
And that was that.
That was the engagement.
But she was already in Indiana University.
Oh, yes.
And that-- that must have been around 1951.
Although I met her, I think, back in 1950.
This is a picture of my parents.
Of course, we saved no pictures.
The three of us, myself and the two sisters who survived, had no pre-war pictures at all.
However, my parents had very dear friends in Israel, which was Palestine in the interwar period.
And after the war, people started sending us pictures of our family.
Well, this is one of them.
This is my mother, Pola Laks, and my father, Isaac Laks.
And I suppose this picture was taken in the middle '30s, in Poland.
This is a picture of the extended family and really both sides of the family.
The central person here is [NON-ENGLISH], grandmother Tenenblum, sitting in the middle.
As you could see, she's wearing a wig, because she was religious.
To her left is my mother, holding a little baby girl.
And that is my older sister, Anna, meaning my sister Krys and I weren't even around.
On the other side of grandmother is my aunt Anjia, my mother's sister.
Now right above Anjia that I just mentioned, on the side of Grandma, are the first on-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the first one is my father's sister.
And next to her is my mother's sister, Roza.
Then you have my father, Isaac.
Then you have my favorite aunt Regina, who is mother's sister.
And then you have my father's sister, [POLISH].
In other words, on each end in the top row, you have my father's two sisters.
In the center, you have my father.
And in the middle on the top row, you have my mother's two sisters.
And this picture should have been taken in the '20s, 1920s in Poland.
Well, this is what you call a picture before and after.
The black and white picture is pre-war.
It has the three of us, lined up in a certain way that we are trying to duplicate now practically every year.
When we meet, we stand up in that formation.
The tallest one, of course, is my oldest sister, Anna.
The middle one is Krys.
And the little one there, that's me.
And that picture was taken in the '30s.
Now on the right to that picture is a present-day photo.
I think it was taken a year or two ago.
From-- adjacent to the old picture, that's myself, Regina Gelb.
In the center is Anna Wilson from Canada.
And on the right of Anna is Krys Lerman.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we're very close to this day.
This is a picture of a-- the Hebrew school in Schlachtensee Berlin DP camp.
Picture was taken in 1946, while I attended that school.
As you could see, the top row, the tallest kids, that's us, the so-called high school group.
The rest are the other kids who lived at the DP camp at the same time as I did.
And there is an arrow here that points directly to where I am located in this picture, wearing a white blouse, open white blouse, over a jacket.
[POINTS] This DP camp was very much oriented toward the-- reestablishing a childhood in a lot of us who have suffered through the war.
And I have absolutely the fondest memories of that school.
And from here, we left for America.
And I spent here not quite a year.
As a matter of fact, about two or three years ago, our high school group had a reunion in Pennsylvania.
And we met all of us.
Of course, this is 50 years later-- no, not 50 years-- 48 years later.
And it was really wonderful having met all these people who came from everywhere.
And, and we were reminiscing.

And interestingly enough, everybody had just about the fondest memories of, of, of the relationships between the kids and the teachers and, and between-- and the whole setup, that was to compensate for our misery during the war, was just absolutely indescribable.
Well, this is my diploma from Indiana University, where I graduated in February, 1953.
And of course, Victor came.
My husband, Victor, came to my graduation.
And it was just absolutely wonderful.

And again, I have the most wonderful memories of my time in Indiana, of my educational experience, of my living in the Midwest-- away from all that preceded my coming there-- and also being away from New York, which is very much European, having found myself for the first time in a real American setting and loving it.
Even though Bloomington was a college community exclusively, I still found myself, as I used to call it, inhaling America, inhaling through America at Bloomington.
And this is my graduation diploma, of which I'm very proud.
Well, this is a family of my-- a picture of my own family, during one of our greater family reunions, of which we always have many.
We have-- we have reunions at the drop of a hat.
Well, this is one of these reunions where all the sisters and the husbands and the children come together, and we have great fun.
Well, anyhow, this was taken in 1991.
At the top is Victor, my husband.
Next to him, I am.
On the-- below us, going from the left to the right, is our other older son, Harry, who is a lawyer, lives in Manhattan.
His wife Cheryl, who lives in Manhattan with him.
And their little son, Max, who is now six years old.
And the gentleman-- the young fellow who is holding Max is our younger son, Paul, who is a geologist and lives in Albuquerque, New Mexico.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we are, again, as close a family as they come.
And that means extended close family, and I mean this with all the other members of-- all the cousins.
And I think we're making up for all that happened before.
